phenomenon: how agents enforce cooperation when monitoring is incomplete
claim: severe punishment can restore cooperation when monitoring is imperfect, but the efficiency cost depends nonlinearly on enforcement severity.

phenomenon: motivational appeal of control independent of material gain
claim: control over others carries intrinsic utility that shapes behavior regardless of financial incentives, creating inefficient retention of authority.

phenomenon: how selection procedures shape bilateral outcomes when parties can veto
claim: sequential shortlisting outperforms simultaneous veto procedures by reducing manipulation and improving allocative fairness.

phenomenon: coordination on monetary versus reciprocal exchange under conditions allowing both
claim: agents coordinate on money as a coordination device even when non-monetary equilibria offer higher welfare.

phenomenon: optimizing public health subsidy policy under competing risks of under- and over-treatment
claim: aggressive subsidies increase access but dramatically increase wastage through treatment of non-affected populations; diagnostic information improves targeting.

phenomenon: how marriage market concerns suppress career investments in women
claim: unmarried women reduce visible career ambition and effort when peers are aware, to avoid signaling undesirable traits to potential partners.

phenomenon: transmission of political attitudes through international migration networks
claim: exposure to democratic norms through migration to democratic countries facilitates political preferences shifting toward democracy.

phenomenon: capacity to engage fairly with ideologically opposing views
claim: individual differences in receptiveness predict selective exposure to opposing views and more impartial evaluation of opposing arguments.

phenomenon: organizational adaptation by emerging-market firms to institutional disruption
claim: access to international resources enables market internationalization, which is conditioned by temporal context and group affiliation to improve performance.

phenomenon: systematic bias in estimating energy content when products are presented in combination
claim: presence of a reference item anchors judgments; the negative calorie illusion reflects contrast-based adjustment rather than averaging of product attributes.

phenomenon: heterogeneity in depth of recursive social reasoning during language interpretation
claim: populations show heterogeneous depths of pragmatic reasoning, and population-level models mask important individual variation in inference capability.

phenomenon: self-deception maintaining positive self-regard despite engaging in unethical behavior
claim: agents maintain honest self-concepts while benefiting from dishonesty through inattention to standards and cognitive flexibility in categorizing actions.

phenomenon: how environmental cues interact with self-conception to shape evaluation patterns
claim: independent self-concepts trigger contrast effects while interdependent self-concepts trigger assimilation to contextual cues.

phenomenon: appeal of counterfeit luxury goods depending on underlying social motivation
claim: preference for counterfeits is driven by the social function of luxury attitudes; moral concerns only reduce counterfeit preference when value-expression motives dominate.

phenomenon: how brand association shifts the psychological function attitudes serve
claim: branding reverses the primary function of product category attitudes, reducing the persuasive advantage of function-matched appeals at the brand level.

phenomenon: self-regulatory depletion from delegating decisions to agents despite lower cognitive effort
claim: relinquishing decision authority depletes self-regulatory resources more than independent decision-making because it threatens perceived agency and free will.

phenomenon: how semantic framing of a risky prospect affects buyer versus seller valuation asymmetrically
claim: risk-associated frames reduce willingness to pay but not willingness to accept, due to buyers' heightened attention to risk and lowest outcomes.

phenomenon: perception of motion in static visual symbols affecting brand engagement
claim: static logos evoking perceived movement enhance consumer engagement and brand attitudes, especially when motion cues align with brand identity.

phenomenon: resource scarcity sharpening preference differentiation in choice sets
claim: salience of scarcity increases arousal, which polarizes preference between favorite and non-favorite items, concentrating choice share on the preferred option.

phenomenon: subjective time pressure arising from perceived conflict among multiple goals
claim: perceived conflict between goals independently of their number creates subjective time pressure mediated by stress and anxiety, affecting time valuation and allocation.
